revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:ヒメヒコ制]]
'''Hime-Hiko System''' (ヒメヒコ制) is a hypothesized co-leadership governance model believed to have existed in ancient Japan, where a Hime (princess or female leader) and a Hiko (prince or male leader) jointly governed a region.<ref>The concept of the Hime-Hiko system was first proposed by Itsue Takamure in 1938. Takamure described it as follows: "In ancient religious governance, the role of the Hime to convey divine will and the role of the Hiko to execute it were indispensable, leading to the emergence of the Hime-Hiko system." Takamure, Itsue. ''Study of Matrilineal Systems.'' Rironsha, 1955 (first edition, 1938), pp. 69, 362. Refer also to Kuratsuka Teruko's ''Culture of Priestesses'' (Heibonsha, 1979).</ref>
== Overview == According to Itsue Takamure's hypothesis presented in her book ''Study of Matrilineal Systems'' (1938), ancient Japan before and during the formation of the Yamato Kingdom was governed through a division of roles between Hime (female leaders associated with religious rituals, agriculture, and female communities) and Hiko (male leaders associated with military and male groups). These leaders co-governed regions, either collaboratively or through a division of labor. Takamure posited that historical texts such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', and ''[[Fudoki]]'' contain examples of such co-leadership, such as Usa-no-Tsuhiko-no-Mikoto (宇佐津彦命) and Usa-no-Tsuhime (宇佐津姫命) in Usa, Oita Prefecture<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Jimmu's pre-accession record, Kōin year.</ref>, Aso-no-Tsuhiko (阿蘇津彦) and Aso-no-Tsuhime (阿蘇津姫) in Aso, Kumamoto Prefecture<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Keikō's record. The "Twelve Aso Shrines" include six pairs of Hime-Hiko deities, suggesting a lineage of the Hime-Hiko system in the region.</ref>, and similar examples in regions such as Kasa (丹後国), Iga (伊賀国), and Kitsu (常陸国). The ''[[Harima Fudoki]]'' also describes governance by paired Hime and Hiko deities in several areas.<ref>Examples from ''Harima Fudoki'' include Iseno-Tsuhime and Iseno-Tsuhiko in Ise-no in Kasai District, and Tamayashihime and Tamayashihiko in Sanyo District, among others.</ref>
Hime-Hiko governance likely persisted until the Nara period for Hime roles, while Hiko roles transitioned into patriarchal political structures by the 5th or 6th century.<ref>{{Sfn|Ōtsu|2017|p=47}}</ref>
== Local Hime-Hiko Systems == Prior to the establishment of the Yamato Kingdom, some scholars suggest that local governance systems based on elder-younger (e-o-to) relationships may have been precursors to the Hime-Hiko system. For instance, records in the ''Nihon Shoki'' describe pairs like "Elder Uka (兄宇迦)" and "Younger Uka (弟宇迦)" in Uda, Nara Prefecture, and "Elder Shiki (兄磯城)" and "Younger Shiki (弟磯城)" in Shiki. Advocates of the Hime-Hiko system interpret younger siblings such as Younger Uka and Younger Shiki as ritual female leaders, while elder siblings like Elder Uka and Elder Shiki represent military male leaders.
However, this interpretation is debated. Some argue that younger siblings like Younger Uka and Younger Shiki were male, and examples such as Elder Takehi (武彦命) and Younger Takehi (武彦命) highlight that male pairs were also common. Critics caution against equating all elder-younger systems with the Hime-Hiko system.
== Hime-Hiko in Yamatai == The ''[[:en:Records of Wei|Records of Wei]]'' describe the governance of [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamataikoku]] under Himiko (卑弥呼), a female shaman-queen, with her younger brother assisting in political affairs.<ref>''Records of Wei'': "Himiko practiced magic and enchanted the people. She had no husband but was assisted by her younger brother in governing the state."</ref> Subordinate states like {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|qid=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima|1=Tsushima Province}} and {{ill|Iki Province|en|Iki Province|qid=Q1052938|lt=Iki|1=Iki Province}} also had dual officials, such as Hiko (卑狗) as the senior officer and Hinamori (卑奴母離) as the junior officer.
The system of dual governance, involving a female religious leader and a male political leader, resembles the Hime-Hiko system. Scholars like Kenzo Torigoe liken this structure to the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|qid=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom|1=Ryukyu Kingdom}}'s dual system of the [[High Priestess of Ryukyu|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and the secular ruler, the {{ill|King of Ryukyu|en|King of Ryukyu|qid=Q1336751|lt=Ryukyu King|1=King of Ryukyu}}.<ref>Torigoe, Kenzo. ''Complete Analysis of the Wa People and Wa Country Traditions.'' Kadokawa Sophia Bunko, 2020, pp. 270-271.</ref>
== Hime-Hiko Shrines == The ''[[Engishiki]]'' lists 35 shrines where Hime and Hiko deities are enshrined together (see table below).<ref>Examples include Tatsuta Hime Shrine and Tatsuta Hiko Shrine in Yamato Province, and Ikomatsu Hime and Ikomatsu Hiko Shrines also in Yamato Province.</ref>
{| class="wikitable" width="100%" ! Shrine Pair (Hime) ! Shrine Pair (Hiko) ! Reading ! Region |- | Tatsuta Hime Shrine | Tatsuta Hiko Shrine | Tatsuta | Yamato Province, Heguri District |- | Ikoma-tsu-Hime Shrine | Ikoma-tsu-Hiko Shrine | Ikoma-tsu | Yamato Province, Heguri District |- | Hakata Hime Shrine | Hakata Hiko Shrine | Hakata | Kawachi Province, Asukabe District |- | Kami-tsu-Hime Shrine | Kami-tsu-Hiko Shrine | Kamitsu | Settsu Province, Toyoshima District |}
Shrines featuring paired Hime and Hiko deities are scattered across Japan, concentrated in regions like Hokuriku, Kinki, Izumo, and Izu to Musashi. Some researchers speculate that paired rulers in Yamatai may have influenced this shrine tradition.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=Itsue Takamure|date=1955|title=Study of Matrilineal Systems|publisher=Rironsha}} * {{Cite book|author=Kenzo Torigoe|date=2020|title=Complete Analysis of the Wa People and Wa Country Traditions|publisher=Kadokawa Sophia Bunko}} * {{Cite book|author=Hiroki Sasaki|date=1992|title=The World of Shamanism|publisher=Kodansha}}
== See Also == * [[Tarihikohime]] * [[Itsue Takamure]]
